summary: Central portfolio should register retail-search as an active MDE product repo with public GitHub URL and upstream dependency on mde.
Phase 1 public readiness is blocked until OpenSearch Cranfield corpus and Cloudflare Worker deployment are configured.

Retail Search is no longer blocked on Cranfield corpus discovery or export; remaining public-readiness blockers are live AWS OpenSearch domain/users, live corpus load/evaluation, and approved Cloudflare deployment.

Retail Search is no longer blocked on OpenSearch provisioning or Cranfield live evaluation; remaining public-readiness blocker is Cloudflare Worker secret configuration, deployment approval, and public endpoint verification.

Retail Search Phase 1 now has a public Cloudflare Worker endpoint backed by live OpenSearch Cranfield data; the next portfolio milestone can move from deployment unblockers to Phase 1 article and Phase 2 BEIR planning.
Retail Search is now directly tryable by non-technical users from the root URL, with indexed-data guidance that explains what Cranfield contains and what searches are likely to work.
Retail Search now explains the query-to-results path visually and through the explain API, improving the educational value of Phase 1 without adding ranking complexity.
Retail Search Phase 1 now exposes evaluation evidence that readers can inspect, including baseline wins and failures, making the public demo more credible and educational.
Retail Search now has room to grow beyond Phase 1: the root page orients users by phase, and detailed evidence lives on focused subpages instead of one crowded page.
Retail Search public pages now explain unfamiliar benchmark names through official dataset references, making the demo easier for readers outside information retrieval to understand.
Retail Search now has a formal traceability chain so future readers can navigate from mission to article, experiment, architecture version, decision, tag, endpoint, and benchmark evidence.

Retail Search evaluation now explains failures by retrieval behavior, allowing architecture candidates to be accepted, rejected, or deferred using query-level evidence instead of aggregate metrics alone.
